result:
Light is a quiet conspirator. It slips through the cracked shutter at 5:17 a.m., lands on the wall like a silver postage stamp, and suddenly the room is no longer mine. It belongs to the birds outside, to the baker two streets over whose laughter I can almost taste, to the memory of a dream that evaporates the instant I try to name it. I lie still, pretending to be asleep so the day won’t notice I’m awake and demand my share of courage.  

Yesterday I found a marble under the couch, a cat’s-eye relic from a childhood I never lived. I rolled it across the floor; it wobbled, steadied, then rolled back as if tethered by an invisible string to the past that isn’t mine. I pocketed it anyway, a talisman against the tyranny of calendars.  

We collect hours like souvenir spoons, display them in velvet-lined drawers, polish the ones that gleam hardest. The dull ones we pretend we never bought. Yet every spoon curves toward the same small mouth, the same eventual hunger.  

Tonight I’ll walk the dog along the creek where the streetlights end. The dark there is generous; it lets you borrow your own shape for a while. Off-leash, the dog becomes a rumor of fur and joy, looping through the reeds, returning only when I whisper the secret name we share. I watch her dash ahead, a comet against the black, and think: courage is just refusing to close the shutter, even when you know the light will come.

message.content:
Light is a quiet conspirator. It slips through the cracked shutter at 5:17 a.m., lands on the wall like a silver postage stamp, and suddenly the room is no longer mine. It belongs to the birds outside, to the baker two streets over whose laughter I can almost taste, to the memory of a dream that evaporates the instant I try to name it. I lie still, pretending to be asleep so the day won’t notice I’m awake and demand my share of courage.  

Yesterday I found a marble under the couch, a cat’s-eye relic from a childhood I never lived. I rolled it across the floor; it wobbled, steadied, then rolled back as if tethered by an invisible string to the past that isn’t mine. I pocketed it anyway, a talisman against the tyranny of calendars.  

We collect hours like souvenir spoons, display them in velvet-lined drawers, polish the ones that gleam hardest. The dull ones we pretend we never bought. Yet every spoon curves toward the same small mouth, the same eventual hunger.  

Tonight I’ll walk the dog along the creek where the streetlights end. The dark there is generous; it lets you borrow your own shape for a while. Off-leash, the dog becomes a rumor of fur and joy, looping through the reeds, returning only when I whisper the secret name we share. I watch her dash ahead, a comet against the black, and think: courage is just refusing to close the shutter, even when you know the light will come.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
